name: Zinc
oneliner: Mild acne benefit. Cap at 40 mg/d to avoid copper depletion.
headline: Zinc

evidence.note: Modest reduction in lesion count over 12 weeks. Adjunct, not monotherapy.
Less effective than oral antibiotics, with cleaner side-effect profile.
Real benefit when zinc is genuinely low. Test before supplementing.
Marketing only. Treats deficiency, not vague complaints.

forms.note: Best-tolerated form. The default for skin endpoints.
Cheaper, well-studied for acne. Often used in older trials.
Pleasant taste; good for lozenges. Adequate for skin.
Highest GI upset risk. Older formulations only.

faq.a: 15–30 mg/d with food, ideally not on an empty stomach. Cap at 40 mg/d total dietary + supplement to avoid copper depletion. For acne, the published RCTs use 30 mg/d for 12 weeks.

faq.q: Why does it upset my stomach?
faq.a: Zinc is gastric-irritant on its own. Always take with a meal that contains some fat. If still problematic, switch from sulfate to picolinate or citrate, and split the dose AM/PM.
Yes. Zinc reduces absorption of tetracyclines (including minocycline and doxycycline used for acne) and quinolones. Separate by at least two hours.

faq.q: When can I expect to see acne improvement?
faq.a: Eight to twelve weeks at minimum. Zinc is a slow, modest player — useful as part of a stack alongside topical retinoids and BPO, rarely effective on its own for moderate-severe presentations.
